  4. Add a row here so the next person reuses it.

See also: design-patterns.md, solid-rules.md, code-smells.md, and the Module Map in CLAUDE.md.

docs.title: Circular Import Rules
docs.text: Status: Mandatory. Apply before planning any new module, class, or cross-module type hint. These rules are enforced by scripts/no_type_checking.py on every commit.

docs.title: Why circular imports are architectural failures
docs.text:
A circular import is not a Python triviality — it is a symptom that two modules have been assigned responsibilities that should live in a third, lower-level module that neither of them owns.

When A imports B and B imports A, Python's import system loads one of them in an incomplete state. The runtime consequence varies — sometimes ImportError, sometimes a None attribute, sometimes silent wrong behavior — and the cause is invisible in normal stack traces. More importantly, the circular dependency means the modules cannot be tested, versioned, or deployed independently.

The three root causes (every circular import is one of these):

  1. Shared type placed in the wrong module. A defines Foo but B needs it; A also needs something from B, creating the cycle. Fix: move Foo to a neutral module C below both.

  2. Bidirectional knowledge. A knows B's public interface and B knows A's public interface. This means neither module has a single clear abstraction boundary. Fix: introduce a Protocol so one side depends only on the abstraction.

  3. Feature Envy. Strategy S accesses Host._private, Host.backend, Host.telemetry. The strategy is doing the host's job. Fix: expose the needed surface via a Protocol that the host satisfies; the strategy depends only on that Protocol.

docs.title: BANNED workarounds
docs.text:
These approaches are forbidden in src/nala/athomic/. They silence the symptom and hide the design failure — the cycle still exists at runtime.

Workaround Why it is banned if TYPE_CHECKING: from X import Y Imports only at type-check time. At runtime, the name is undefined. The cycle still exists; mypy pretends it doesn't. from __future__ import annotations Defers ALL annotation evaluation to strings. The cycle still exists at import time for any non-annotation use. Zero architectural value. KNOWN_OPEN whitelists A list that says "we know this is broken" is not a fix. Every entry in the list is technical debt that actively degrades the layer boundary enforcement. String-quoted types: Optional["Foo"] Same as TYPE_CHECKING — defers evaluation without fixing the cycle.

docs.title: Fix 1 — Extract a Protocol into a neutral module
docs.text:
When to use: A depends on B and B depends on A because A calls methods on B (or vice versa), and neither can be the "lower" layer.

Pattern:

# Before (circular):
# A imports B for type hint only
# B imports A to use it as a parameter type

# After:
# neutral/protocol.py  ← new, imported by both A and B
class BProtocol(Protocol):
    def needed_method(self) -> str: ...

# A uses BProtocol, no longer imports B directly
# B implements BProtocol implicitly (structural subtyping) — never imports A

Rules: - The Protocol lives in a module below both A and B in the layer order. - The Protocol is the minimum surface the caller needs — ISP applies here. If callers only use 2 of 5 methods, split the Protocol. - B never imports A. B simply satisfies the Protocol structurally. - The composition root (factory/wiring) is the only place that knows both A and B.

Real example from this codebase (pending fix): - producers/strategies/protocol.py imports BaseProducer from producers/base.py - producers/base.py imports PublishingStrategyProtocol from strategies/protocol.py - Fix: extract ProducerTransportProtocol with only the surface strategies actually use (backend, service_name, tracer, payload_processor, key_header_serializer, telemetry_adapter, dispatch_bytes). Strategies depend on ProducerTransportProtocol. BaseProducer satisfies it structurally. Neither imports the other.

docs.title: Fix 2 — Move shared types to a neutral module
docs.text:
When to use: The cycle exists only because a dataclass, enum, or named tuple is defined in A but also needed by B, and A needs something from B.

Pattern:

# Before:
# A defines FleetSnapshot — B needs it — A also needs something from B

# After:
# shared/snapshots.py  ← pure data, no service imports
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class FleetSnapshot:
    pressure: float
    latency_p99: float

# A imports FleetSnapshot from shared/snapshots — no longer imports B
# B imports FleetSnapshot from shared/snapshots — no longer imports A

Rules: - The neutral module contains only data — dataclasses, enums, TypedDicts, NamedTuples. - It imports nothing from any service or infrastructure module. - It lives at the lowest applicable layer so both A and B can import it without violating layer order.

Real example from this codebase (pending fix): - pressure/composer.py → algorithms/telemetry.py → pressure/fleet.py → pressure/__init__.py → pressure/composer.py - Fix: extract pressure/snapshots.py with FleetPressureSnapshot and PressureIndex. Both sides of the cycle import from snapshots.py only.

docs.title: Fix 3 — Restructure the dependency direction
docs.text:
When to use: There is no real bidirectional dependency — one side has a redundant or incorrect import. The lower-level module imported something that it does not actually need from the higher-level one.

Pattern:

# Before:
# base.py imports ConsumptionStrategyProtocol just for a type hint in a method signature
# ConsumptionStrategyProtocol imports BaseConsumer

# After:
# base.py uses a more abstract type already available at its layer (e.g., BaseService)
# ConsumptionStrategyProtocol keeps its own parameter type narrow

Rules: - Determine the actual call direction. Usually one import is unnecessary. - Use the most abstract type available that satisfies the real contract. - Never import upward in the layer order. The layer graph is a DAG.

Real example from this codebase (pending fix): - retry/processor_service.py imports BaseConsumer for a type hint where BaseService (already at a lower layer) is sufficient.

docs.title: Decision tree: "I need to reference type X from module A, but X is in module B, which imports A"
docs.text:
START: Module A needs to reference type X from module B, but B also imports A.

1. Does A only need X for a type hint (parameter, return, attribute)?
   └─ YES → Does B's import of A cross a layer boundary or create Feature Envy?
              ├─ YES (B accesses A's internals / private members)
              │   └─ EXTRACT A PROTOCOL (Fix 1): surface only what B needs from A,
              │      place the Protocol below both in the layer order.
              └─ NO (B just needs a data shape from A)
                  └─ Is X a pure data type (dataclass, enum, NamedTuple)?
                      ├─ YES → MOVE X TO NEUTRAL MODULE (Fix 2):
                      │         place it below both A and B.
                      └─ NO  → RESTRUCTURE DIRECTION (Fix 3):
                                 check whether B genuinely needs A's concrete type
                                 or whether a more abstract type already below B suffices.

2. Does A need X at runtime (instantiation, method call)?
   └─ This is Feature Envy. A is doing B's work.
      → EXTRACT A PROTOCOL (Fix 1): A depends on the Protocol; B satisfies it.
        The concrete wiring happens at the composition root (factory).

3. None of the above apply?
   └─ The import is wrong. Remove it and redesign.

docs.title: Layer order (one-way only)
docs.text:
facade → ai → workflows → streaming → integration → http → plugins
      → performance → security → resilience → database
      → serializer → observability → config

Every arrow is one-way. A lower-level module never imports from a higher-level one. If you find yourself needing to do so, the type or abstraction belongs in a lower layer.

docs.title: Checklist before adding any cross-module type reference
docs.text:
• [ ] Does the import respect layer order (higher → lower only)?
  • [ ] Is the referenced type a Protocol or pure data type?
  • [ ] Could the type live in a neutral module below both sides?
  • [ ] Does the import create any cycle (direct or transitive)?
  • [ ] Is this a genuine need, or Feature Envy (accessing private members)?
  • [ ] Is TYPE_CHECKING anywhere in this file? If yes, stop and apply Fix 1, 2, or 3.

docs.title: Enforcement
docs.text:
scripts/no_type_checking.py runs on every commit via pre-commit hooks. It: - Parses all *.py files in src/nala/athomic/ via Python AST. - Rejects any if TYPE_CHECKING: block not in KNOWN_OPEN. - Prints the exact remediation steps (this document).

Do not add entries to KNOWN_OPEN. Every entry is existing debt. The goal is to reduce KNOWN_OPEN to zero and then delete the whitelist entirely.

docs.title: Code Smells — Athomic
docs.text:
"If it stinks, change it." — Kent Beck's grandmother

Detect: make smells (full report) / make smells-summary (counts only). Detector: scripts/check_smells.py — always exits 0, advisory only.

Current state (baseline 2026-06): 756 smells across 5 categories. Fix them incrementally. Track progress with make smells-summary.

A code smell is not a bug. It's a structural weakness that signals a deeper problem — code that grew without discipline, or OOP applied incorrectly, or coupling that makes change painful. Each smell has a canonical refactoring from Fowler.

docs.title: CATEGORY 1: BLOATERS
docs.text: Code that grows without discipline. Usually accumulate over time when no one refactors.

docs.title: SMELL-B01 — Long Method (219 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: A function or method longer than 50 lines.

Why it stinks: A 100-line method has more than one responsibility. It's impossible to test all branches, impossible to read without a map, impossible to reuse any part of it.

Real instances:

197 lines  resilience/adaptive_concurrency/controller.py:149  def __init__
150 lines  ai/features/base.py:160                            def encode_batch
125 lines  resilience/adaptive_concurrency/controller.py:1073 def _build_telemetry

Refactoring: Extract Method

# ❌ BEFORE — 150 lines doing everything inline
async def encode_batch(self, raw_states):
    # normalize...
    # validate...
    # check cache...
    # execute...
    # write cache...
    # record metrics...

# ✅ AFTER — each concern is a named stage
async def encode_batch(self, raw_states):
    return await self._decorated.encode_tensor_batch(raw_states)
    # each decorator handles one concern

docs.title: SMELL-B02 — Large Class / God Class (6 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: A file with 500+ lines. At 800+ lines it's a god class.

Why it stinks: One class that knows everything = one class you're afraid to touch. Every feature touches it. It's the epicenter of merge conflicts and regressions.

Real instances:

1267 lines  resilience/adaptive_concurrency/controller.py  ← CRITICAL
 818 lines  database/kvstore/base.py
 598 lines  ai/features/caching/proxy.py

Refactoring: Extract Class

# ❌ BEFORE — AdaptiveConcurrencyController owns:
# AIMD algorithm + DRF fairness + Slot management + Admission queue + Telemetry building

# ✅ AFTER — one file, one responsibility:
# controller.py      ← orchestrates only
# algorithms/aimd.py ← AIMD calculation only
# fairness/drf.py    ← DRF fairness only
# telemetry.py       ← metrics assembly only

docs.title: SMELL-B03 — Long Parameter List (67 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: Functions with more than 5 parameters (excluding self/cls).

Why it stinks: Callers must know the order. Adding a parameter breaks every caller. Five or more parameters means the function probably has too many jobs.

Real instances:

13 params  performance/cache/handlers/cache_handler.py:32  def __init__
13 params  integration/messaging/decorators/consumer.py    def subscribe_to
 9 params  ai/features/base.py:84                         def __init__

Refactoring: Introduce Parameter Object

# ❌ BEFORE
def __init__(self, key_prefix, ttl, compress_above_bytes, compress_level,
             max_payload_bytes, dtype, semantic_version, epoch, vector_id,
             cache_provider, metrics_enabled, coalescing, backoff_base): ...

# ✅ AFTER — already the pattern in this codebase
def __init__(self, settings: FeatureCacheSettings, vector_id: str) -> None: ...

docs.title: SMELL-B04 — Primitive Obsession (248 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: Using primitive types (dict, str, int) for data that has structure and behavior — things that should be objects.

Why it stinks: No validation at the boundary. No domain language. Business rules scattered across the codebase instead of encapsulated in a type.

Real instances:

param 'parameters: Dict[str, Any]' in execute_query() — should be QueryParameters
param 'parameters: Dict[str, Any]' in run_query() — same

Refactoring: Replace Primitive with Object

# ❌ BEFORE
def execute_query(cypher: str, parameters: Dict[str, Any]) -> List[Dict]: ...

# ✅ AFTER
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class GraphQuery:
    cypher: str
    parameters: dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        if not self.cypher.strip():
            raise ValueError("Empty Cypher query")

def execute_query(query: GraphQuery) -> List[Dict]: ...

docs.title: SMELL-B05 — Data Clumps (32 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: The same group of 3+ parameters appearing together in 4+ functions. They're a clump — they want to become a class.

Why it stinks: If you need to add a field to the "group", you must update every function signature. The group has no name, no validation, no documentation.

Refactoring: Introduce Parameter Object

# ❌ BEFORE — (host, port, timeout) appear in 8 different functions
def create_client(host: str, port: int, timeout: float) -> Client: ...
def connect(host: str, port: int, timeout: float) -> None: ...
def ping(host: str, port: int, timeout: float) -> bool: ...

# ✅ AFTER
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ConnectionConfig:
    host: str
    port: int
    timeout: float

def create_client(config: ConnectionConfig) -> Client: ...

docs.title: CATEGORY 2: OOP ABUSERS
docs.text: Incomplete or incorrect application of object-oriented principles.

docs.title: SMELL-O01 — Switch Statements / isinstance Chains (19 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: A function with 3+ isinstance() calls dispatching to different behavior.

Why it stinks: Every time you add a new type, you must find all dispatch points and add a new branch. This is an Open/Closed Principle violation — the code is not closed for modification.

Real instances:

23 isinstance() calls  serializer/base.py     (worst)
10 isinstance() calls  ai/features/caching/proxy.py
 8 isinstance() calls  event_sourcing/stores/kv_snapshot_store.py

Refactoring: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism (Strategy + Registry)

# ❌ BEFORE — Open for modification at every new type
def serialize(value):
    if isinstance(value, str): return value.encode()
    elif isinstance(value, int): return str(value).encode()
    elif isinstance(value, dict): return json.dumps(value).encode()
    # ... adding float? Must modify this function

# ✅ AFTER — Open for extension via Registry
class SerializerProtocol(Protocol):
    def serialize(self, value: Any) -> bytes: ...

_REGISTRY: dict[type, SerializerProtocol] = {
    str: StringSerializer(),
    int: IntSerializer(),
    dict: JsonSerializer(),
}

def serialize(value: Any) -> bytes:
    handler = _REGISTRY.get(type(value))
    if handler is None:
        raise TypeError(f"No serializer for {type(value)}")
    return handler.serialize(value)
# Adding float? Just: _REGISTRY[float] = FloatSerializer()

docs.title: SMELL-O02 — Mutable Default Argument
docs.text:
Definition: def f(x=[]) or def f(x={}).

Why it stinks: Python creates the default ONCE when the function is defined. All calls share THE SAME object. Appending to it corrupts all future calls.

# ❌ WRONG — x is shared between all callers
def process(items: list = []):
    items.append("new")
    return items

process()  # ["new"]
process()  # ["new", "new"]  ← BUG!

# ✅ CORRECT
def process(items: list | None = None) -> list:
    if items is None:
        items = []
    items.append("new")
    return items

docs.title: SMELL-O03 — Temporary Field
docs.text:
Definition: An instance variable that is set only inside a conditional in a business method, but never declared in __init__. When the condition is false, the attribute doesn't exist — leading to AttributeError at runtime.

Note: Lazy initialization in lifecycle methods (_connect, setup) is NOT this smell.

Refactoring: Introduce Null Object or declare with None default

# ❌ WRONG — self._buffer only exists after process() is called
class Processor:
    def __init__(self): pass  # _buffer not declared here!

    def process(self, data):
        if data:
            self._buffer = []  # Temporary field!
            self._buffer.extend(data)

    def flush(self):
        return self._buffer  # AttributeError if process() never called!

# ✅ CORRECT
class Processor:
    def __init__(self):
        self._buffer: list = []  # Always exists, safe default

    def process(self, data):
        if data:
            self._buffer.extend(data)

docs.title: CATEGORY 3: CHANGE PREVENTERS
docs.text: When a change in one place forces changes everywhere else.

docs.title: SMELL-C01 — Divergent Change
docs.text:
Definition: One class that changes for many different reasons (SRP violation). Symptom: importing from 12+ different modules = too many masters.

Why it stinks: You touch the class for a cache change, a logging change, a validation change, a schema change. It's a change magnet.

Refactoring: Extract Class — split by reason to change.

docs.title: SMELL-C02 — Shotgun Surgery (5 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: A single change requires modifications in many different files. Symptom: a non-infrastructure symbol imported by 20+ files.

Real instances:

KVStoreProtocol    imported by 37 files
DecisionContext    imported by 37 files
connection_manager_factory  imported by 25 files

Refactoring: Move Method / Consolidate

# If changing KVStoreProtocol forces changes in 37 files,
# either the protocol is too fine-grained (needs versioning),
# or the 37 files should go through a Facade instead of depending directly.

docs.title: CATEGORY 4: DISPENSABLES
docs.text: Pointless code whose absence would make things cleaner.

docs.title: SMELL-D01 — Excessive Comments (3 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: Files where > 35% of non-blank lines are comments.

Why it stinks: A comment is an apology for unclear code. If you need 3 paragraphs to explain what a method does, the method name and structure are wrong. (Exception: legal headers, complex math with formula references.)

Refactoring: Rename to make code self-documenting

# ❌ BEFORE — needs a comment
# Check if the elapsed time exceeds our allowed processing window
if time.time() - self.start > self.max_seconds:
    ...

# ✅ AFTER — name carries the meaning
if self._processing_window_exceeded():
    ...

docs.title: SMELL-D02 — Duplicate Code
docs.text:
Definition: The same code block appearing in multiple places.

Why it stinks: Fix a bug in one copy, forget the others. The codebase now has multiple versions of the "truth". Dijkstra: "if you have two things, you have two different things."

Refactoring: Extract Function

# ❌ WRONG — same normalization logic in 3 methods
def encode(self, states):
    normalized = [s if isinstance(s, dict) else s.dict() for s in states]
    ...
def encode_batch(self, states):
    normalized = [s if isinstance(s, dict) else s.dict() for s in states]
    ...

# ✅ CORRECT — one function, one truth
# normalize_states() already exists in ai/features/types.py

docs.title: SMELL-D03 — Data Class (7 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: A class that only holds data (fields + getters), has no behavior.

Why it stinks: The behavior that operates on this data is scattered across the codebase. The class is just a dumb container; it offers no abstraction.

Refactoring: Move Behavior Here, or use @dataclass / Pydantic

# ❌ BEFORE — dumb container
class GraphQueryResult:
    def __init__(self):
        self.rows: list = []
        self.columns: list[str] = []
        self.execution_time: float = 0.0

    # No behavior — just stores data

# ✅ CORRECT OPTION A — use @dataclass if it's just data
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class GraphQueryResult:
    rows: list[dict]
    columns: list[str]
    execution_time: float

    def is_empty(self) -> bool:          # ← behavior belongs HERE
        return len(self.rows) == 0

    def to_dataframe(self): ...          # ← behavior belongs HERE

docs.title: SMELL-D04 — Dead Code
docs.text:
Definition: Variables assigned but never read, methods never called.

Why it stinks: Readers waste time understanding code that does nothing. Dead code rots — it's never updated when surrounding code changes.

Refactoring: Delete it. Version control is your safety net.

docs.title: SMELL-D05 — Lazy Class (46 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: A concrete class with fewer than 2 public methods and under 40 lines. It doesn't do enough to justify existing as a class.

Refactoring: Inline Class or replace with a function

# ❌ BEFORE — a class for a single function
class TimeoutCalculator:
    def calculate(self, base: float, multiplier: float) -> float:
        return base * multiplier

# ✅ AFTER — just a function
def calculate_timeout(base: float, multiplier: float) -> float:
    return base * multiplier

docs.title: SMELL-D06 — print() in Production (10 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: print() calls in src/ (outside tests).

Why it stinks: Bypasses the structured logging pipeline. Invisible in production. No log level, no trace ID, no structured context.

Refactoring:

# ❌ WRONG
print(f"Processing batch of {len(items)} items")

# ✅ CORRECT
from loguru import logger
logger.debug("Processing batch", count=len(items))

docs.title: CATEGORY 5: COUPLERS
docs.text: Excessive coupling between classes.

docs.title: SMELL-P01 — Inappropriate Intimacy (67 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: A class accessing the private attributes (_name) of another class.

Why it stinks: It couples two classes at their implementation level, not their interface level. Change the private attribute → the other class breaks.

Real instances:

causality/vector_clock.py  → Accessing incoming_vc._clock
database/manager.py        → Accessing s._run_loop

Refactoring: Move Method or expose via Protocol

# ❌ WRONG — VectorClock reads another VectorClock's internals
class VectorClock:
    def merge(self, other: "VectorClock") -> None:
        for key, ts in other._clock.items():  # intimate!
            ...

# ✅ CORRECT — expose what's needed via a public method
class VectorClock:
    def items(self) -> Iterable[tuple[str, int]]:
        return self._clock.items()

    def merge(self, other: "VectorClock") -> None:
        for key, ts in other.items():  # clean interface
            ...

docs.title: SMELL-P02 — Message Chains (2 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: A long chain of calls: a.b().c().d().e() (5+ levels deep).

Why it stinks: You're navigating an object graph manually. If the middle changes, every chain breaks. Violates Law of Demeter: "only talk to your immediate neighbors."

Refactoring: Hide Delegate

# ❌ WRONG
result = self._engine._plan._offsets[feature_name][0]

# ✅ CORRECT — ask for what you need, not how to navigate to it
result = self._plan.get_offset(feature_name)

docs.title: SMELL-P03 — Middle Man (1 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: A class where most methods just delegate to another object. It adds indirection without adding value.

Refactoring: Remove Middle Man or Inline Method

# ❌ WRONG — FeatureProxy just forwards everything
class FeatureProxy:
    def encode(self, state): return self._service.encode(state)
    def encode_batch(self, states): return self._service.encode_batch(states)
    def get_dimension(self, vid): return self._service.get_dimension(vid)
    # Why does this class exist?

# ✅ CORRECT — remove the proxy, use the service directly
# OR make the proxy add real value (caching, auth, rate limiting)

docs.title: SMELL-P04 — Bare/Broad except (24 in codebase)
docs.text:
Definition: except: or except Exception: with no logging and no re-raise. Errors disappear silently.

Refactoring: Log or catch specific type

# ❌ WRONG — error disappears
try:
    result = await self._client.get(key)
except Exception:
    pass

# ✅ CORRECT
try:
    result = await self._client.get(key)
except ConnectionError as e:
    logger.warning("Cache unavailable", key=key, error=str(e))
    return None
except Exception:
    logger.exception("Unexpected cache error", key=key)
    raise

docs.title: Smell → Refactoring Quick Reference
docs.text: Code Smell Refactoring B01 Long Method Extract Method B02 Large Class / God Class Extract Class B03 Long Parameter List Introduce Parameter Object B04 Primitive Obsession Replace Primitive with Object B05 Data Clumps Introduce Parameter Object O01 Switch Statements (isinstance) Replace Conditional with Polymorphism O02 Mutable Default Argument Use None + initialize in body O03 Temporary Field Declare with None default in init C01 Divergent Change Extract Class C02 Shotgun Surgery Move Method / Facade D01 Excessive Comments Rename + restructure D02 Duplicate Code Extract Function D03 Data Class Move Behavior / use @dataclass D04 Dead Code Delete D05 Lazy Class Inline Class / function D06 print() in prod Replace with logger.* P01 Inappropriate Intimacy Move Method / Protocol P02 Message Chains Hide Delegate P03 Middle Man Remove / Inline Method P04 Bare except Catch specific type + log

docs.title: Not statically detectable (require human review)
docs.text: Smell Why undetectable How to detect manually Feature Envy Needs type data to know which class's data a method uses Method that takes 3+ params from another class's fields Refused Bequest Needs runtime usage data Subclass that overrides parent and calls pass or raise NotImplementedError Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces Semantic similarity beyond syntax Two classes with similar fields but different method names for same concept Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies Requires full class hierarchy map Every new subclass of A requires a new subclass of B Speculative Generality Needs usage data Abstract class or method with exactly 1 concrete implementation Incomplete Library Class Domain-specific judgment You're extending a stdlib/third-party class just to add one method

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• SOLID architecture rules: docs/guides/architecture/solid-rules.md
  • Design patterns: docs/guides/architecture/design-patterns.md
  • Violations backlog: docs/backlog/todo/solid-violations.md

docs.title: Design Patterns — Athomic
docs.text: Reference for which patterns are in use, where they live, and when to apply each one. Before implementing a feature, identify which pattern(s) apply. Patterns are not optional style choices — they are the correct solution to recurring structural problems.

docs.title: Pattern vs Anti-Pattern: The Core Rule
docs.text:
A design pattern solves a recurring problem by establishing a proven structure. An anti-pattern is what you get when you solve the problem ad-hoc:

Anti-pattern Correct Pattern if isinstance(x, A): ... elif isinstance(x, B): ... Strategy + Registry Constructor with 10+ parameters Builder or Parameter Object One giant class with 50 methods Facade + Extract Class Direct new ConcreteClass() everywhere Factory Method Copy-paste code with slight variations Template Method Module importing from every other module Dependency Inversion + Protocol

docs.title: Patterns In Use — With Their Location
1. Factory Method
docs.text:
What it solves: Decouples object creation from usage. Callers ask for an object by name, the factory decides which concrete class to instantiate.

Where it is:

ai/features/engines/factory.py          ← EngineFactory: "numpy" → AsyncNumpyEngine
ai/features/transformations/registry.py ← TransformationRegistry: "minmax" → MinMaxTransformation
ai/features/encoders/factory.py         ← FeatureEncoderFactory (5-step assembly)
database/factory.py                     ← connection_manager_factory
database/kvstore/factory.py             ← KVStoreFactory
integration/messaging/producers/factory.py
resilience/locking/factory.py           ← LockingFactory

The pattern in this codebase:

# ✅ CORRECT — factory + registry
class EngineFactory:
    def create(self, settings: EngineSettings) -> FeatureExecutionEngineProtocol:
        engine_cls = EngineRegistry.get(settings.engine)  # lookup by string key
        return engine_cls(settings)

# ✅ CORRECT — new engine registered without touching factory
EngineRegistry.register("ray", RayEngine)

When to apply: Any time you need to create objects whose concrete type is determined at runtime (from config, settings, or registry key).

Anti-pattern to avoid:

# ❌ WRONG — direct instantiation scattered everywhere
if settings.engine == "numpy":
    engine = AsyncNumpyEngine(settings)
elif settings.engine == "torch":
    engine = TorchEngine(settings)
# Adding "ray" requires modifying THIS code AND finding all other places

docs.title: 2. Registry
docs.text:
What it solves: Maps string keys to implementations. Combined with Factory, enables extension without modification (OCP).

Where it is:

ai/features/engines/registry.py                  ← EngineRegistry
ai/features/transformations/registry.py          ← TransformationRegistry
ai/features/resilience/adaptive_concurrency/discipline/registry.py
ai/features/resilience/adaptive_concurrency/policies/registry.py

Pattern:

class TransformationRegistry:
    _registry: dict[str, type[BaseFeatureTransformation]] = {}

    @classmethod
    def register(cls, key: str, transformation_cls: type) -> None:
        cls._registry[key] = transformation_cls

    @classmethod
    def get(cls, key: str) -> type[BaseFeatureTransformation]:
        if key not in cls._registry:
            raise KeyError(f"No transformation registered for key '{key}'")
        return cls._registry[key]

    @classmethod
    def register_defaults(cls) -> None:
        cls.register("minmax", MinMaxTransformation)
        cls.register("zscore", ZScoreTransformation)
        ...

When to apply: When the set of implementations is open-ended and should be extensible by adding new registrations rather than modifying existing code.

docs.title: 3. Strategy
docs.text:
What it solves: Encapsulates a family of interchangeable algorithms. The context uses the algorithm through a Protocol, not a concrete class.

Where it is:

resilience/adaptive_concurrency/algorithms/      ← AIMD, Gradient2 strategies
resilience/adaptive_concurrency/discipline/      ← FIFO, LIFO queue strategies
resilience/adaptive_concurrency/policies/        ← reject, probabilistic overload strategies
integration/messaging/consumers/strategies/      ← consumption, orchestration strategies
integration/messaging/producers/strategies/      ← direct, delayed strategies

Pattern:

# Protocol defines the algorithm interface
class QueueDisciplineProtocol(Protocol):
    def push(self, item: RequestSlot) -> None: ...
    def pop(self) -> RequestSlot: ...
    def peek(self) -> RequestSlot | None: ...

# Strategies implement the Protocol
class FIFODiscipline:
    def push(self, item: RequestSlot) -> None:
        self._queue.append(item)
    def pop(self) -> RequestSlot:
        return self._queue.popleft()

class LIFODiscipline:
    def push(self, item: RequestSlot) -> None:
        self._stack.append(item)
    def pop(self) -> RequestSlot:
        return self._stack.pop()

# Context uses Protocol — doesn't know which concrete strategy it holds
class AdmissionQueue:
    def __init__(self, discipline: QueueDisciplineProtocol) -> None:
        self._discipline = discipline

When to apply: When a class needs to support multiple variants of the same behavior, and those variants should be interchangeable at runtime.

Anti-pattern to avoid:

# ❌ WRONG — behavior switch via flag or isinstance
class AdmissionQueue:
    def pop(self):
        if self._mode == "fifo":
            return self._queue.popleft()
        elif self._mode == "lifo":
            return self._stack.pop()
        # OCP violated: adding "priority" requires modifying this class

docs.title: 4. Decorator (Structural)
docs.text:
What it solves: Adds behavior to an object by wrapping it, without subclassing. Each decorator adds one concern. The core is clean.

Where it is:

ai/features/encoders/decorator_stack.py     ← EncoderDecoratorStack
ai/features/encoders/telemetry.py           ← TelemetryInstrumentedEncoder
ai/features/encoders/lineage.py             ← LineageTrackingEncoder
ai/features/encoders/admission.py           ← AdmissionGuardedEncoder
ai/features/caching/proxy.py               ← CachedTransformation (wraps any transformer)

The stack in this codebase:

TelemetryInstrumentedEncoder     ← outermost: OTel spans + Prometheus
  └── LineageTrackingEncoder     ← OpenLineage governance events
       └── AdmissionGuardedEncoder ← pre-checks memory before allocation
            └── CachedEncoder    ← batch-level cache (optional)
                 └── BackpressureAwareEncoder ← throttle (optional)
                      └── FeatureEncoderService  ← core: no cross-cutting concerns

Pattern:

class TelemetryInstrumentedEncoder:
    def __init__(self, inner: FeatureEncoderProtocol) -> None:
        self._inner = inner  # the next decorator or the core

    async def encode_tensor_batch(self, raw_states) -> FeatureResponse:
        with self._tracer.start_span("feature.encode"):
            result = await self._inner.encode_tensor_batch(raw_states)
            self._histogram.observe(time.perf_counter() - start)
            return result

When to apply: Cross-cutting concerns (logging, metrics, caching, security checks) that should be composable and independent of the core logic.

docs.title: 5. Protocol (Structural Subtyping)
docs.text:
What it solves: Defines a contract without coupling to an implementation. Enables Dependency Inversion across layers.

Where it is: Every layer boundary in this codebase.

ai/features/engines/protocol.py             ← FeatureExecutionEngineProtocol
ai/features/transformations/protocol.py     ← TransformerProtocol
ai/features/imputation/protocol.py          ← FeatureImputationProtocol
database/kvstore/protocol.py                ← KVStoreProtocol
resilience/locking/protocol.py              ← LockingProtocol
integration/messaging/consumers/protocol.py ← ConsumerProtocol

Critical rule: The Protocol is defined at the lower layer. The higher layer implements it structurally (no inheritance needed).

# ✅ CORRECT — Protocol at the consumer's layer (lower level)
# ai/features/quality/protocol.py
class DriftBaselineProtocol(Protocol):
    def get_feature_stats(
        self, vector_id: str, feature_key: str
    ) -> tuple[float | None, float | None]: ...

# Higher layer implements it without explicit inheritance
class DistributedQualityBaseline:
    # Structurally matches DriftBaselineProtocol — no 'implements' declaration
    def get_feature_stats(self, vector_id, feature_key):
        ...

Anti-pattern to avoid:

# ❌ WRONG — importing the concrete class from a HIGHER layer
# observability/metrics/feature_metrics.py
from nala.athomic.ai.features.quality.protocol import DriftBaselineProtocol
# observability/ depends on ai/ — inverted layer order — DIP violation

docs.title: 6. Template Method
docs.text:
What it solves: Defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, letting subclasses override specific steps without changing the structure.

Where it is:

ai/features/transformations/base.py    ← BaseFeatureTransformation
ai/features/transformations/async_base.py ← AsyncBaseFeatureTransformation
athomic/services/base.py              ← BaseService (lifecycle)

Pattern:

class BaseFeatureTransformation(Generic[T_in]):
    # Template method — defines the structure, delegates to _execute_transform_batch
    async def transform_batch(self, values: Sequence[T_in]) -> TensorResult:
        if len(values) >= MIN_BATCH_THRESHOLD:
            return await self._run_in_process_pool(values)
        return await self._run_in_thread(values)

    # Abstract step — subclasses provide the actual logic
    @abstractmethod
    def _execute_transform_batch(self, values: Sequence[T_in]) -> TensorResult: ...

When to apply: When multiple classes share the same algorithm structure but differ in one or two steps.

docs.title: 7. Facade
docs.text:
What it solves: Provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem. Clients use the Facade; they never reach into subsystem internals.

Where it is:

athomic/facade.py                         ← Athomic (the composition root)
ai/features/service.py                    ← FeaturePlatformService (ai layer facade)

Pattern:

# ✅ CORRECT — clients use FeaturePlatformService, not internal encoders
service = FeaturePlatformService(settings)
response = await service.encode("user_risk_v1", raw_state)

# ❌ WRONG — reaching into internals
registry = service._registry  # bypasses the facade contract
encoder = registry.get("user_risk_v1")  # internal — may change
result = await encoder._engine.execute(plan, context)  # internal

docs.title: 8. Observer (Event-Driven variant)
docs.text:
What it solves: Notifies multiple subscribers when a state change occurs, without the publisher knowing who's listening.

Where it is:

integration/messaging/producers/       ← Kafka producers (event publishers)
integration/messaging/consumers/       ← Kafka consumers (event subscribers)
database/outbox/                       ← Transactional outbox pattern

The outbox variant:

# Publish event atomically with the business transaction (outbox stores to Mongo)
await outbox.append(event)
# Background worker reads outbox → publishes to Kafka → consumers react
# Publisher and consumers are fully decoupled

docs.title: When to Choose Which Pattern
docs.text: Situation Pattern Need to create objects by string key Factory Method + Registry Multiple variants of an algorithm Strategy Adding cross-cutting concerns (logging, cache, auth) Decorator Decoupling layers (import direction) Protocol (DIP) Same algorithm structure, different steps Template Method Simplify complex subsystem API Facade React to events without tight coupling Observer / Event Bus Group related config params Parameter Object (dataclass) Build complex objects step by step Builder

docs.title: Anti-Patterns Banned in This Codebase
docs.text: Anti-pattern Why banned Correct pattern isinstance chain (3+) OCP violation Strategy + Registry TYPE_CHECKING import Hides broken architecture Move to correct layer Direct new ConcreteClass() in business logic DIP violation Factory + Protocol 10+ parameter constructors Long Parameter List smell Config dataclass God class (>800 lines) SRP violation Extract Class print() in production Bypasses observability logger.* Bare except: pass Swallows errors Specific exception + log

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• SOLID rules: docs/guides/architecture/solid-rules.md
  • Code smells: docs/guides/architecture/code-smells.md
  • Violations backlog: docs/backlog/todo/solid-violations.md

docs.title: SOLID Architecture Rules — Athomic
docs.text: Status: MANDATORY. Every file written or reviewed must comply with these rules. Violations are tracked in docs/backlog/todo/solid-violations.md.

docs.title: Why This Document Exists
docs.text:
A TYPE_CHECKING block was found in observability/metrics/usage/ai/features/feature_metrics.py importing from ai.features.quality.protocol. The observability layer — which is infrastructure — was depending on the AI domain layer, inverting the dependency arrow. This is a DIP violation and it couples two modules that must be independent.

TYPE_CHECKING is never acceptable in this codebase. It is a cover-up for an architectural problem, not a solution.

docs.title: The 5 SOLID Principles — Applied to This Codebase
S — Single Responsibility Principle
docs.text:
Each module has one reason to change.

Module Its reason to change observability/metrics/ Prometheus metric definitions change ai/features/encoders/ Feature encoding logic changes ai/features/encoders/telemetry.py Drift recording logic changes database/ Storage backend changes resilience/ Resilience policy changes

Violation indicator: A module that imports from 3+ unrelated layers has too many reasons to change.

# ❌ WRONG — feature_metrics.py mixing metric definitions with domain logic
class DriftStatsProvider(Protocol): ...        # domain concern
def record_output_distribution(...): ...       # AI domain logic
feature_encoding_duration_seconds = Histogram  # metric definition

# ✅ CORRECT
# feature_metrics.py  → ONLY metric object definitions
# encoders/telemetry.py → recording logic (owns AI domain types)

docs.title: O — Open/Closed Principle
docs.text:
Extend via Protocols and Registries, never by modifying core.

# ❌ WRONG — adding a branch to handle a new case
def execute(engine):
    if isinstance(engine, NumpyEngine):
        ...
    elif isinstance(engine, TorchEngine):  # modifying core
        ...

# ✅ CORRECT — register a new provider, core never changes
class TorchEngine(FeatureExecutionEngineProtocol):
    async def execute_tensor_batch(...): ...

EngineRegistry.register("torch", TorchEngine)

docs.title: L — Liskov Substitution Principle
docs.text:
Every Protocol implementation must be fully substitutable.

# ❌ WRONG — checking the concrete type breaks substitutability
def compress(provider: CompressionProtocol) -> bytes:
    if isinstance(provider, GzipProvider):  # violates LSP
        return provider.gzip_compress(data)
    return provider.compress(data)

# ✅ CORRECT — call the Protocol method, trust the contract
def compress(provider: CompressionProtocol) -> bytes:
    return provider.compress(data)

docs.title: I — Interface Segregation Principle
docs.text:
Protocols expose only the methods the consumer needs.

# ❌ WRONG — importing a fat class for one method
from nala.athomic.ai.features.quality.protocol import DriftBaselineProtocol
# ^ 10 methods, we need 1

# ✅ CORRECT — define a minimal protocol at the consumer's layer
class DriftStatsProvider(Protocol):
    def get_feature_stats(self, vector_id: str, feature_key: str) -> Tuple[Optional[float], Optional[float]]: ...

docs.title: D — Dependency Inversion Principle (THE MOST CRITICAL)
docs.text:
High-level modules NEVER depend on low-level modules. Both depend on abstractions.

The abstraction (Protocol) is defined at the lower layer, not the higher one.

docs.title: Layer Order — LOW → HIGH (higher layers MAY import lower, NEVER the reverse)
docs.text:
observability/      ← ABSOLUTE BOTTOM — zero imports from any Athomic module
config/schemas/     ← Pure Pydantic — zero imports from any Athomic module
resilience/         ← may import: observability, config
security/           ← may import: observability, config, resilience
database/           ← may import: observability, config
performance/        ← may import: observability, config, database, resilience
serializer/         ← may import: observability, config
integration/        ← may import: observability, config, database, serializer, security, resilience, performance
streaming/          ← may import: observability, config, database, integration
event_sourcing/     ← may import: observability, config, database
workflows/          ← may import: observability, config, database, integration, ai
plugins/            ← may import: observability, config — NEVER facade
ai/                 ← may import: observability, config, database, resilience, performance
facade/             ← COMPOSITION ROOT — imports everything, wires everything
api/                ← may import: facade, observability

docs.title: DIP Violation Examples
docs.text:
# ❌ FORBIDDEN — observability importing AI domain
# observability/metrics/feature_metrics.py
from nala.athomic.ai.features.quality.protocol import DriftBaselineProtocol

# ❌ FORBIDDEN — plugins importing the composition root
# plugins/hooks.py
from nala.athomic.facade import Athomic

# ❌ FORBIDDEN — database importing AI
# database/mongo.py
from nala.athomic.ai.features.types import FeatureDict

# ✅ CORRECT — AI importing observability (low layer)
# ai/features/encoders/telemetry.py
from nala.athomic.observability.metrics.usage.ai.features import feature_output_distribution

# ✅ CORRECT — define Protocol at the low layer so AI can implement it without coupling
# observability/metrics/some_module.py
class SomeStatsProvider(Protocol):
    def get_stats(self, key: str) -> float: ...

docs.title: 1. TYPE_CHECKING — BANNED COMPLETELY IN THIS CODEBASE
docs.text:
# ❌ NEVER — this is a gambiarra that hides a broken architecture
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from nala.athomic.ai.features.quality.protocol import DriftBaselineProtocol

Why it's wrong: - The import only runs during type checking, masking a real circular/inverted dependency at runtime - It creates invisible coupling: the module "doesn't import" at runtime but the architecture is wrong - Mypy still validates the type — so the coupling is real

The real fix: Move the code to the correct layer OR define a local Protocol.

# ✅ CORRECT option A — move the function UP to the layer that owns the domain type
# ai/features/encoders/telemetry.py
from ..quality.protocol import DriftBaselineProtocol  # same layer, correct direction

# ✅ CORRECT option B — define a minimal Protocol at the lower layer
# observability/metrics/some_module.py
class SomeStatsProvider(Protocol):
    def get_stats(self, key: str) -> float: ...
# The higher layer implements it structurally — no coupling needed

docs.title: 2. observability/ importing from any other Athomic module — BANNED
docs.text:
# ❌ FORBIDDEN — in any file under observability/
from nala.athomic.ai.features.quality.protocol import X
from nala.athomic.database.kvstore.protocol import Y
from nala.athomic.resilience.backpressure.manager import Z

observability/ must be importable in complete isolation. It is the foundation.

docs.title: 3. config/schemas/ importing from any other Athomic module — BANNED
docs.text:
# ❌ FORBIDDEN — config schemas are pure Pydantic, no runtime deps
from nala.athomic.ai.features.types import FeatureValue

docs.title: 4. plugins/ importing from facade/ — BANNED
docs.text:
# ❌ FORBIDDEN
from nala.athomic.facade import Athomic

# ✅ CORRECT — define AthomicProtocol in a shared interfaces/ module
# plugins/interfaces.py
class AthomicProtocol(Protocol):
    def get_component(self, name: str) -> Any: ...

docs.title: 5. Any module importing from a higher layer — BANNED
docs.text:
# ❌ FORBIDDEN — resilience importing AI
from nala.athomic.ai.features.something import SomeThing

# ❌ FORBIDDEN — database importing resilience
from nala.athomic.resilience.circuit_breaker import CircuitBreaker

docs.title: 6. Circular imports (within the same package) — BANNED
docs.text:
Using TYPE_CHECKING to paper over circular imports is doubly banned.

# ❌ WRONG — circular: A imports B, B imports A, workaround with TYPE_CHECKING
# module_a.py
if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from .module_b import B

# ✅ CORRECT — extract shared types to module_c that both A and B import
# shared_types.py  ← new file, no imports from A or B
class SharedBase(Protocol): ...

# module_a.py
from .shared_types import SharedBase

# module_b.py
from .shared_types import SharedBase

docs.title: Checklist Before Writing Any Import
docs.text:
1. Is this module at the observability or config layer? → it imports nothing from other Athomic modules.
  2. Does this import go from a lower layer to a higher layer? → STOP. Invert via Protocol.
  3. Am I about to write TYPE_CHECKING? → STOP. Restructure the architecture.
  4. Is this a circular import within the same package? → Extract shared types to a third module.

docs.title: Quick Layer Check
docs.text:
# When writing: from nala.athomic.X import Y
# Ask: is my current file in a LOWER layer than X?
# If yes → FORBIDDEN. Define a Protocol here instead.

docs.title: Code Smell Detector
docs.text:
A pre-commit hook runs scripts/check_solid.py to catch violations before commit. It detects: - TYPE_CHECKING usage anywhere in the codebase - observability/ importing from other nala.athomic.* modules - config/schemas/ importing from other nala.athomic.* modules - plugins/ importing from facade

Run manually:

python scripts/check_solid.py

docs.title: Module-Specific Rules
docs.text: Module Can import from Cannot import from Rule observability/ stdlib, prometheus, loguru, opentelemetry Any nala.athomic.* SOLID-002 config/schemas/ stdlib, pydantic Any nala.athomic.* SOLID-003 resilience/ observability, config ai, facade SOLID-005 security/ observability, config, resilience ai, integration, performance, facade SOLID-008 database/ observability, config ai, resilience, facade SOLID-006 performance/ observability, config, database, resilience ai, integration, facade SOLID-009 serializer/ observability, config ai, database, resilience, facade — integration/ observability, config, database, serializer, security, resilience, performance ai, facade SOLID-007 streaming/ observability, config, database, integration ai, facade SOLID-010 event_sourcing/ observability, config, database ai, facade SOLID-011 workflows/ observability, config, database, integration, ai facade SOLID-012 plugins/ observability, config facade, ai, database SOLID-004 ai/ observability, config, database, resilience, performance facade, api — facade/ Everything Nothing (it IS the root) — api/ facade, observability Direct lower-layer modules —

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Violations backlog: docs/backlog/todo/solid-violations.md
  • Memory rule (persists across sessions): .claude/projects/.../memory/solid_architecture_rules.md

docs.title: Graph Database (Neo4j)
docs.text:
The athomic.database.graph module provides a high-performance, asynchronous interface for interacting with Graph Databases. Currently, it ships with a robust Neo4j implementation.

It is designed to support complex relationship mapping, such as Data Lineage, Impact Analysis, and Knowledge Graphs.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Async-First: Built on top of the official neo4j asynchronous driver.
  • Observability: Automatic OpenTelemetry tracing (spans) and Prometheus metrics for every query executed.
  • Connection Groups: Supports multiple named connections (e.g., default, analytics) via ConnectionManager.
  • Resilience: Integrated connection pooling and automatic retry mechanisms managed by the driver.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Configure the graph connection in your settings.toml. The module uses the standard ConnectionGroup pattern found in other database modules.

[default.database.graph]
# The default connection to use when none is specified
default_connection_name = "default_graph"

[default.database.graph.connections.default_graph]
enabled = true
backend = "neo4j"

[default.database.graph.connections.default_graph.provider]
backend = "neo4j"
# Connection URI (supports bolt://, neo4j://, neo4j+s://)
uri = "bolt://localhost:7687"
user = "neo4j"
password = "password" # pragma: allowlist secret
max_connection_pool_size = 50
max_connection_lifetime = 3600

docs.title: Dependency Injection (Recommended)
docs.text:
The best way to interact with the Graph DB is by injecting the GraphDatabaseProtocol via the ConnectionManager or the specific Factory.

from nala.athomic.database.graph.protocol import GraphDatabaseProtocol
from nala.athomic.database.factory import connection_manager_factory

async def get_service_dependencies():
    # 1. Get the manager
    connection_manager = connection_manager_factory.create()

    # 2. Get the client (uses 'default_connection_name' if name is omitted)
    graph_client: GraphDatabaseProtocol = connection_manager.get_graph_db()

    # 3. Execute a Cypher query
    query = """
    MATCH (s:Service)-[:DEPENDS_ON]->(d:Database)
    WHERE s.name = $service_name
    RETURN d.name as database, d.type as type
    """

    results = await graph_client.execute_query(
        query, 
        parameters={"service_name": "payment-service"}
    )

    for record in results:
        print(f"Database: {record['database']}, Type: {record['type']}")

docs.title: Multiple Connections
docs.text:
If you have configured multiple connections (e.g., one for Lineage, one for Fraud Detection), you can request them by name:

# Accessing the 'fraud_graph' connection configured in settings.toml
fraud_graph = connection_manager.get_graph_db("fraud_graph")

docs.title: The Protocol
docs.text: The module exposes GraphDatabaseProtocol. Your domain logic should depend on this protocol, not on Neo4jProvider directly. This ensures strict Dependency Inversion (DIP).

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
You do not need to add manual logging or timing code. The BaseGraphStore wrapper automatically:

  1. Starts a Trace Span named graph_db.execute_query.
  2. Records Prometheus Metrics:
    • nala_graph_db_operations_total{status="success|failure"}
    • nala_graph_db_operation_duration_seconds

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Driver-specific exceptions (like neo4j.exceptions.Neo4jError) are logged and propagated. In infrastructure layers, we wrap critical connection errors in ServiceConnectionError to standardize health checks.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This guide provides practical recipes and patterns for using the Vector Store module in real-world scenarios, such as implementing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), semantic search with filtering, and multi-tenant data isolation.

docs.title: Recipe 1: Indexing Documents for RAG
docs.text:
This recipe demonstrates how to chunk a text document, generate embeddings using an embedding model, and store them with metadata for later retrieval.

import uuid
from typing import List

from nala.athomic.ai.embeddings import EmbeddingModelProtocol
from nala.athomic.database.factory import connection_manager_factory
from nala.athomic.database.vector.types import VectorRecord

async def index_content(
    content: str, 
    metadata: dict, 
    embedding_model: EmbeddingModelProtocol
):
    # 1. Get the Vector Store client
    store = connection_manager_factory.create().get_vector_store()

    # 2. Chunk the content (Simple overlap strategy)
    chunk_size = 1000
    overlap = 100
    chunks = [
        content[i : i + chunk_size] 
        for i in range(0, len(content), chunk_size - overlap)
    ]

    # 3. Generate Embeddings in Batch
    # It is much more efficient to embed multiple texts at once
    vectors = await embedding_model.embed_documents(chunks)

    # 4. Create Vector Records
    records = []
    for chunk, vector in zip(chunks, vectors):
        record = VectorRecord(
            id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
            vector=vector,
            payload={
                "content": chunk,
                "source": metadata.get("source"),
                "author": metadata.get("author"),
                "created_at": metadata.get("created_at")
            }
        )
        records.append(record)

    # 5. Upsert to the 'knowledge_base' collection
    # Ensure the collection exists first (idempotent operation)
    await store.create_collection("knowledge_base", dimension=1536)

    await store.upsert("knowledge_base", records)
    print(f"Indexed {len(records)} chunks.")

docs.title: Recipe 2: Semantic Search with Metadata Filtering
docs.text:
This recipe shows how to perform a semantic search while restricting results to specific criteria (e.g., only documents from a specific author).

async def search_knowledge_base(query: str, author_filter: str = None):
    store = connection_manager_factory.create().get_vector_store()
    embedding_model = ... # Get your embedding model

    # 1. Convert the user query into a vector
    query_vector = await embedding_model.embed_query(query)

    # 2. Build filters (if applicable)
    # The dictionary format allows providers to translate to their native query language
    filters = {}
    if author_filter:
        filters["author"] = author_filter

    # 3. Execute Search
    results = await store.search(
        collection="knowledge_base",
        vector=query_vector,
        limit=5,
        score_threshold=0.8, # Only relevant matches
        filter=filters
    )

    # 4. Process Results
    for hit in results:
        print(f"Score: {hit.score:.4f} | Content: {hit.payload['content'][:100]}...")

docs.title: Recipe 3: Implementing Multi-Tenancy
docs.text: One of the strongest features of the Athomic Vector Store is its seamless support for multi-tenancy via the ContextAwareVectorStore wrapper.

docs.title: Automatic Injection
docs.text:
If you have the context_aware wrapper enabled in your configuration, you do not need to pass the tenant ID manually. The system extracts it from the current execution context.

# In a FastAPI request handler or Task
from nala.athomic.context import context_vars

async def handle_request(user_query: str):
    # Assume context middleware has already set this
    # context_vars.set_tenant_id("tenant-123")

    store = connection_manager_factory.create().get_vector_store()

    # This search is AUTOMATICALLY scoped to "tenant-123".
    # Data from other tenants will strictly NOT be visible.
    results = await store.search("documents", vector=...)

docs.title: Manual Override (Admin Mode)
docs.text:
If you are writing an admin tool or a background job that needs to access a specific tenant's data explicitly, you can bypass the context:

async def admin_audit_tenant(target_tenant_id: str):
    store = connection_manager_factory.create().get_vector_store()

    # Explicitly passing 'tenant' overrides the context variable
    results = await store.search(
        "documents", 
        vector=..., 
        tenant=target_tenant_id
    )

docs.title: 1. Collection Management
docs.text:
• Dimension Matching: Always ensure the dimension passed to create_collection matches exactly the output dimension of your embedding model (e.g., OpenAI text-embedding-3-small is 1536).
  • Naming Convention: Use snake_case for collection names. Avoid using tenant IDs in collection names; use the tenant partitioning feature instead for better scalability.

docs.title: 2. Error Handling
docs.text:
The Vector Store methods can raise specific exceptions that you should handle: * CollectionNotFoundError: If you try to search in a collection that hasn't been created. * ServiceConnectionError: If the vector database is down.

from nala.athomic.services.exceptions import ServiceConnectionError

try:
    await store.search(...)
except ServiceConnectionError:
    # Implement fallback logic (e.g., basic keyword search in DB)
    return await keyword_search(...)

docs.title: 3. Batching Upserts
docs.text: Avoid calling upsert in a loop for single records. Always batch your records into lists (e.g., 100-500 records per call) to reduce network overhead and improve throughput.

docs.title: Test Conventions
docs.text: Comprehensive reference for writing tests in this repository. Applies to all test types: unit, integration, benchmark, and e2e. Valid for both human engineers and AI assistants.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Directory Layout
  2. Test Settings — The Golden Rule
  3. Settings Helpers Reference
  4. Fixture Scope Guidelines
  5. Markers
  6. Async Tests
  7. Mocking Conventions
  8. Integration Test Patterns
  9. Benchmark Tests
  10. Running Tests
  11. Checklist Before Committing a New Test File

docs.title: Directory Layout
docs.text:
tests/
├── conftest.py              ← root conftest: shared fixtures + settings helpers
├── unit/                    ← fast, no I/O, no infrastructure
│   └── nala/athomic/...
├── integration/             ← require running infra (Docker Compose)
│   └── nala/athomic/...
├── benchmark/               ← pytest-benchmark performance suite
│   └── nala/athomic/...
├── e2e/                     ← end-to-end flows
└── settings/                ← TOML settings files (one per test file)
    ├── unit/...
    ├── integration/...      ← (same sub-tree as the test files)
    ├── benchmark/...
    └── ...

Every test file that needs settings has a companion TOML in tests/settings/ at the exact same relative path, with the .py extension replaced by .toml:

tests/integration/nala/athomic/ai/llm/test_int_ollama.py
  → tests/settings/integration/nala/athomic/ai/llm/test_int_ollama.toml

tests/benchmark/nala/athomic/ai/features/test_benchmark_feature_encoding.py
  → tests/settings/benchmark/nala/athomic/ai/features/test_benchmark_feature_encoding.toml

docs.title: Test Settings — The Golden Rule
docs.text:
Never instantiate domain configuration objects inline in test code.

The following are forbidden in test files:

# ❌ WRONG — instantiating config objects inline
_VECTOR_SETTINGS = FeatureVectorSettings(
    vector_id="benchmark_v1",
    features=[
        FeatureDefinitionSettings(name="age_scaled", source_key="age", transformation="min_max", ...),
    ],
)
_ENGINE_SETTINGS = FeatureEngineConnectionSettings(backend="async_numpy", ...)

The correct approach is:

  1. Define configuration in a TOML file at tests/settings/<same path>/<test-name>.toml
  2. Load it via load_test_settings() / settings_loader fixture in a fixture
  3. Read it with get_settings() inside the test or fixture that needs it
# ✅ CORRECT — settings come from the TOML companion file

# tests/settings/benchmark/nala/athomic/ai/features/test_benchmark_feature_encoding.toml
[default.ai.features.vectors.benchmark_v1]
vector_id = "benchmark_v1"
[[default.ai.features.vectors.benchmark_v1.features]]
name = "age_scaled"
source_key = "age"
transformation = "min_max"
params = { min_val = 0.0, max_val = 100.0 }

# test file
from tests.conftest import load_test_settings, clear_test_settings
from nala.athomic.config.settings import get_settings

def setup_module(module):
    load_test_settings("tests/settings/benchmark/.../test_benchmark_feature_encoding.toml")
    vector_settings = get_settings().ai.features.vectors["benchmark_v1"]
    ...

Why? Test TOML files are: - Version-controlled and reviewable alongside the code - Consistent with how production settings are loaded (same dynaconf stack) - Isolated per test — no shared mutable Python objects - Easy to diff when settings change

docs.title: Settings Helpers Reference
docs.text:
All helpers are defined in tests/conftest.py and available everywhere in the test suite without import in most cases (fixtures). For non-fixture contexts (setup_module, etc.) import explicitly:

from tests.conftest import load_test_settings, clear_test_settings

docs.title: load_test_settings(toml_path, **extra_env)
docs.text:
Sets NALA_SETTINGS_FILES, clears the get_settings LRU cache, and reloads the settings module. Call at the start of a fixture's setup phase.

@pytest.fixture(scope="module", autouse=True)
def setup_settings():
    load_test_settings("tests/settings/integration/.../test_foo.toml")
    yield
    clear_test_settings()

Pass additional env vars as keyword arguments:

load_test_settings("tests/settings/.../test_foo.toml", ENV_FOR_DYNACONF="testing")

docs.title: clear_test_settings(*extra_keys)
docs.text:
Removes NALA_SETTINGS_FILES (and any extra keys) from os.environ and clears the settings cache. Call in the teardown phase (after yield).

def teardown_module(module):
    clear_test_settings()                          # removes NALA_SETTINGS_FILES
    clear_test_settings("ENV_FOR_DYNACONF")        # also removes extra vars

docs.title: settings_loader fixture (function-scoped)
docs.text:
A pytest fixture that wraps load_test_settings with monkeypatch for automatic cleanup — no explicit teardown needed. Use this for function-scoped fixtures.

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def setup_settings(settings_loader):
    settings_loader("tests/settings/unit/.../test_foo.toml")
    # no yield needed — monkeypatch restores env automatically

Rule: Use settings_loader fixture for function-scoped needs. Use load_test_settings / clear_test_settings for module-scoped and setup_module contexts (because monkeypatch is function-scoped by default).

docs.title: Fixture Scope Guidelines
docs.text:
Scope When to use Notes "function" Default. Most unit tests. monkeypatch works; full isolation per test "module" Integration tests — expensive setUp (LLM/DB/Kafka connects) load_test_settings + yield + clear_test_settings "session" Config checks, environment probing (e.g. ollama_available) Never mutate state; read-only

Module-scoped settings fixture template:

@pytest.fixture(scope="module", autouse=True)
def setup_settings():
    load_test_settings("tests/settings/integration/nala/athomic/.../test_foo.toml")
    yield
    clear_test_settings()

Function-scoped settings fixture template:

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def setup_settings(settings_loader):
    settings_loader("tests/settings/unit/nala/athomic/.../test_foo.toml")

docs.title: Markers
docs.text:
Register markers in pytest.ini. Current markers:

Marker Description Run with unit Pure unit test make test (default) integration Requires infra make test-integration RUN_INTEGRATION=true e2e End-to-end flow make test-all benchmark pytest-benchmark perf suite make benchmark ai Requires LLM / external AI call --run-ai flag

Mark every file at module level:

pytestmark = [pytest.mark.unit]
# or
pytestmark = [pytest.mark.integration, pytest.mark.asyncio(loop_scope="module"), pytest.mark.ai]

docs.title: Async Tests
docs.text:
• Async mode is auto (asyncio_mode = "auto" in pytest.ini) — no @pytest.mark.asyncio decorator needed on individual tests.
  • For module-scoped async fixtures add loop_scope="module" to both the pytestmark and each async fixture:
pytestmark = [pytest.mark.asyncio(loop_scope="module")]

@pytest_asyncio.fixture(scope="module", loop_scope="module")
async def provider():
    ...
    yield
    ...
  • For benchmarks, async code must be bridged to sync via an explicit event loop (asyncio.new_event_loop() + loop.run_until_complete()), because pytest-benchmark requires a sync callable. See Benchmark Tests for the pattern.

docs.title: Mocking Conventions
docs.text:
• Prefer MagicMock(spec=SomeProtocol) — spec= ensures the mock only exposes attributes the real object has, catching typos at test time.
  • Use AsyncMock for coroutines (async def methods, __aenter__, __aexit__).
  • Patch at the usage site, not the definition site:
# The code under test imports from nala.athomic.config; patch there:
with patch("nala.athomic.config.get_settings") as mock_get_settings:
    ...
  • Do not mock the database in unit tests that test DB interaction — use integration tests with a real database instead.

docs.title: Checking service availability
docs.text:
@pytest_asyncio.fixture(scope="module", loop_scope="module")
async def ollama_available() -> bool:
    try:
        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=2.0) as client:
            response = await client.get("http://localhost:11434")
            return response.status_code == 200
    except httpx.RequestError:
        return False

docs.title: Skipping when unavailable
docs.text:
@pytest_asyncio.fixture(scope="module", loop_scope="module")
async def provider(ollama_available: bool):
    if not ollama_available:
        pytest.skip("Ollama not reachable — skipping.")
    ...

docs.title: Full integration test file skeleton
docs.text:
# tests/integration/nala/athomic/some/test_int_foo.py
import pytest
import pytest_asyncio
from tests.conftest import clear_test_settings, load_test_settings
from nala.athomic.config.settings import get_settings

pytestmark = [
    pytest.mark.integration,
    pytest.mark.asyncio(loop_scope="module"),
]

@pytest.fixture(scope="module", autouse=True)
def setup_settings():
    load_test_settings("tests/settings/integration/nala/athomic/some/test_int_foo.toml")
    yield
    clear_test_settings()

@pytest_asyncio.fixture(scope="module", loop_scope="module")
async def service():
    svc = MyService(settings=get_settings().some)
    await svc.start()
    yield svc
    await svc.stop()

async def test_something(service):
    result = await service.do_thing()
    assert result is not None

docs.title: Benchmark Tests
docs.text: Benchmark tests live in tests/benchmark/ and require the pytest-benchmark plugin. They are excluded from make test and must be run explicitly.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
1. Mark with pytestmark = [pytest.mark.benchmark].
  2. Load settings from a companion TOML (same rules as all other tests).
  3. Use setup_module / teardown_module for one-time engine setup (avoids benchmark overhead on every iteration).
  4. For async engines: create ONE event loop at module level and use loop.run_until_complete() — do not use asyncio.run() per iteration (it creates a new loop and breaks asyncio primitives that bind to the first loop, such as asyncio.Lock / asyncio.Queue).
  5. Build context columns as 2D (N, 1) arrays for scalar features when feeding AsyncNumpyEngine._execute_tensor_batch_impl directly — the engine's output matrix slice is (N, k) shaped.

docs.title: Benchmark file skeleton
docs.text:
# tests/benchmark/nala/athomic/.../test_benchmark_foo.py
from __future__ import annotations
import asyncio
import pytest
from tests.conftest import clear_test_settings, load_test_settings
from nala.athomic.config.settings import get_settings

pytestmark = [pytest.mark.benchmark]

_SETTINGS_TOML = "tests/settings/benchmark/nala/athomic/.../test_benchmark_foo.toml"

_loop: asyncio.AbstractEventLoop
_engine: ...   # your engine

def setup_module(module: object) -> None:
    global _loop, _engine
    load_test_settings(_SETTINGS_TOML)
    settings = get_settings()
    _loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
    _engine = MyEngine(settings=settings.something)
    _loop.run_until_complete(_engine.connect())

def teardown_module(module: object) -> None:
    _loop.run_until_complete(_engine.close())
    _loop.close()
    clear_test_settings()

@pytest.mark.parametrize("batch_size", [1, 32, 128, 512])
def test_foo_latency(benchmark, batch_size):
    def _run():
        return _loop.run_until_complete(_engine.do_work(batch_size))
    result = benchmark(_run)
    assert result is not None

docs.title: Running benchmarks
docs.text:
make benchmark                      # run without saving
make benchmark-save NAME=baseline   # capture baseline JSON
make benchmark-compare              # compare against saved baseline

Results are stored under .tests_saves/benchmark/ in pytest-benchmark JSON format with p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput, and mean statistics.

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
make test                                    # unit tests only (default)
make test-integration RUN_INTEGRATION=true   # integration tests (requires infra)
make test-all RUN_INTEGRATION=true           # unit + integration
make coverage                                # unit tests + terminal coverage report
make benchmark                               # benchmark suite

# Single file
poetry run pytest tests/unit/path/to/test_file.py

# With verbose output + stdout
make td tests/unit/path/to/test_file.py      # -s -vv shortcut

# AI-marked tests (LLM calls)
make ta tests/unit/path/to/test_file.py

docs.title: Checklist Before Committing a New Test File
docs.text:
• [ ] Settings are in a TOML companion file at tests/settings/<same path>/<test-name>.toml
  • [ ] No inline instantiation of FeatureVectorSettings, FeatureDefinitionSettings, FeatureCacheSettings, or similar domain config objects in test code
  • [ ] Uses load_test_settings / clear_test_settings (module-scoped) or settings_loader fixture (function-scoped) — never bare os.environ["NALA_SETTINGS_FILES"] = ...
  • [ ] Appropriate marker set via pytestmark
  • [ ] Integration tests check for service availability and skip gracefully
  • [ ] Async tests use pytest_asyncio.fixture + loop_scope="module" where needed
  • [ ] Benchmark tests use a single persistent event loop (asyncio.new_event_loop()), not asyncio.run() per iteration
  • [ ] make test still passes (unit tests unaffected by new integration/benchmark files)

docs.title: Getting Started with Durable Workflows
docs.text: The nala.athomic.workflows module provides an agnostic way to orchestrate long-running business processes. It allows you to write workflow logic once and execute it either on a production-grade engine (Temporal.io) or in-memory for lightning-fast tests (Local).

docs.title: 1. Defining a Workflow
docs.text:
Workflows are defined using the standard Temporal Python SDK decorators. This ensures compatibility with the production engine.

from datetime import timedelta
from temporalio import activity, workflow

# 1. Define Activities (The "Side Effects")
# Activities are where you perform I/O (DB calls, HTTP requests).
@activity.defn
async def charge_user(user_id: str, amount: float) -> str:
    # Logic to call payment gateway...
    return "transaction_id_123"

@activity.defn
async def send_receipt(email: str) -> None:
    # Logic to send email...
    pass

# 2. Define the Workflow (The "Orchestrator")
# Workflows must be deterministic (no random, no system time, no direct I/O).
@workflow.defn(name="PaymentProcessing")
class PaymentWorkflow:
    @workflow.run
    async def run(self, user_id: str, email: str, amount: float) -> dict:
        workflow.logger.info(f"Starting payment for {user_id}")

        # Execute Activity 1: Charge User
        # Automatic retries are handled by the engine here.
        tx_id = await workflow.execute_activity(
            charge_user,
            args=[user_id, amount],
            start_to_close_timeout=timedelta(seconds=10),
        )

        # Execute Activity 2: Send Receipt
        await workflow.execute_activity(
            send_receipt,
            email,
            start_to_close_timeout=timedelta(seconds=5),
        )

        return {"status": "COMPLETED", "transaction_id": tx_id}

docs.title: 2. Registering for Local Execution
docs.text:
When running in Production (Temporal), the Worker automatically scans and registers workflows. However, when running in Local/Test Mode (In-Memory), the LocalWorkflowClient does not scan code. You must explicitly register your workflow function in the in-memory registry.

This is typically done in your conftest.py or application startup logic for dev environments.

from nala.athomic.workflows.providers.local.registry import local_workflow_registry
from my_app.workflows.payment import PaymentWorkflow

# Register the workflow name pointing to the class method
# This allows client.start_workflow("PaymentProcessing", ...) to work locally.
local_workflow_registry.register("PaymentProcessing", PaymentWorkflow().run)

docs.title: 3. Executing a Workflow
docs.text:
To start a workflow, use the agnostic WorkflowClientFactory. This keeps your business logic decoupled from the specific backend (Temporal vs. Local) configured in your settings.toml.

import asyncio
from nala.athomic.workflows.factory import WorkflowClientFactory
from nala.athomic.workflows.models import WorkflowOptions

async def trigger_process():
    # 1. Create the client (Backend is determined by settings.WORKFLOWS.PROVIDER.BACKEND)
    client = WorkflowClientFactory.create()

    # 2. Configure Execution Options
    # Note: 'execution_timeout_seconds' accepts strings like "1h", "30m"
    options = WorkflowOptions(
        workflow_id=f"pay-req-{uuid.uuid4()}",
        task_queue="payments-queue",
        execution_timeout_seconds="1h" 
    )

    # 3. Start Execution
    # The payload (arguments) is automatically serialized, compressed, 
    # and encrypted by the Nala Payload Pipeline before leaving the app.
    execution = await client.start_workflow(
        "PaymentProcessing", # Matches the name in @workflow.defn
        "user_123",          # Arg 1
        "user@example.com",  # Arg 2
        99.90,               # Arg 3
        options=options
    )

    print(f"Workflow started with RunID: {execution.run_id}")

    # 4. (Optional) Wait for result
    result = await client.get_result(execution.workflow_id, run_id=execution.run_id)
    print(f"Result: {result.result}")
